original_abstract:
Finding the maximum independent set (MIS) is a fundamental NP-hard problem with extensive applications in fields ranging from network analysis to computational biology. Currently, the most effective exact solution methods utilize branch-and-reduce frameworks, which combine search trees with powerful reduction rules. While recent research has focused on the development of new reduction rules, other critical components—such as the branching strategy used to select the next vertex—have remained largely unexplored. Traditionally, these solvers rely on simple heuristics, most commonly branching on the vertex with the highest degree.

In this work, we introduce a novel branching strategy guided by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Given the architectural complexity of modern exact solvers, traditional supervised and reinforcement learning approaches face significant challenges regarding label generation and state-transition costs. To circumvent these issues, we employ a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the GNN parameters, optimizing directly for reduced execution time. Our experimental evaluation demonstrates that the GCN-based strategy outperforms the standard maximum degree heuristic across a variety of benchmark instances. Specifically, our approach achieved a speedup on 73% of the tested instances, with a median speedup of 24%, showing that learned branching strategies can significantly enhance the efficiency of state-of-the-art exact MIS solvers.
humanized_abstract:
The Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem is one of the most studied NP-hard problems in the literature. It has many applications in graph theory, as well as in other fields such as social network analysis and computational biology. The state-of-the-art algorithms to solve MIS exactly in polynomial time use a branch-and-reduce framework, which builds search trees on a subset of vertices while applying relevant reduction rules. Most of the recent advances in MIS have focused on designing new reduction rules, while little is known about the impact of branching on the vertex subset to be explored next. Current implementations rely on very simple heuristics, almost universally based on the highest degree vertex.

We present a new branching strategy for constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs) guided by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). While the structures of exact MIS solvers are highly complex, applying conventional supervised learning and reinforcement learning to them is hampered by two obstacles: labeling the training data and navigating the state transition space efficiently. To overcome these obstacles, we apply a population-based genetic algorithm to optimize the parameters of a GNN to directly lead to shorter run times. Experimental results show that the evolved GCN-based strategy outperforms the standard maximum degree (MaxDeg) heuristic on a wide range of instances. The speedup of the evolved strategy over the MaxDeg strategy is 73% in terms of run time, with a median speedup factor of 24. This confirms that a learnt branching strategy can be highly beneficial for state-of-the-art exact MIS solvers.

document.output:
The Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem is one of the most studied NP-hard problems in the literature. It has many applications in graph theory, as well as in other fields such as social network analysis and computational biology. The state-of-the-art algorithms to solve MIS exactly in polynomial time use a branch-and-reduce framework, which builds search trees on a subset of vertices while applying relevant reduction rules. Most of the recent advances in MIS have focused on designing new reduction rules, while little is known about the impact of branching on the vertex subset to be explored next. Current implementations rely on very simple heuristics, almost universally based on the highest degree vertex.

We present a new branching strategy for constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs) guided by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). While the structures of exact MIS solvers are highly complex, applying conventional supervised learning and reinforcement learning to them is hampered by two obstacles: labeling the training data and navigating the state transition space efficiently. To overcome these obstacles, we apply a population-based genetic algorithm to optimize the parameters of a GNN to directly lead to shorter run times. Experimental results show that the evolved GCN-based strategy outperforms the standard maximum degree (MaxDeg) heuristic on a wide range of instances. The speedup of the evolved strategy over the MaxDeg strategy is 73% in terms of run time, with a median speedup factor of 24. This confirms that a learnt branching strategy can be highly beneficial for state-of-the-art exact MIS solvers.
